title: Maximum Subarray Sum
description: Find the contiguous subarray with the maximum sum

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all possible contiguous subarrays by using two nested loops to compute their sums and track the maximum. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Subarray Sum with Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the maximum subarray sum problem. The intuition is straightforward: to find the maximum sum, we must consider every possible contiguous subarray. Since we don't know which subarray will have the maximum sum, we systematically examine all possibilities. We use two nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index of the subarray, and the inner loop extends the subarray to all possible ending indices. For each subarray defined by start and end indices, we compute its sum by adding elements one by one. We maintain a variable to track the maximum sum encountered so far, updating it whenever we find a larger sum. The algorithm begins by initializing max_sum to negative infinity (or the first element) to handle arrays with all negative numbers. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1, we initialize current_sum to 0. Then for each ending index j from i to n-1, we add arr[j] to current_sum, representing the sum of subarray arr[i..j]. After each addition, we compare current_sum with max_sum and update max_sum if current_sum is larger. After checking all subarrays, max_sum contains the answer. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have n starting points and for each, we examine up to n ending points, resulting in roughly n(n+1)/2 operations. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large n but serves as a foundation for understanding more optimized solutions like Kadane's algorithm. Common mistakes include initializing max_sum to 0 (fails for all negative arrays), using incorrect loop bounds, or not resetting current_sum properly. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the key subroutine of computing subarray sums.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Sum of All Subarrays Starting at Index i
ladders.desc: Given a starting index, compute sums of all contiguous subarrays beginning at that index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the maximum subarray sum, we need to examine subarrays starting from each possible position. For a fixed starting index i, we want to compute the sums of all subarrays that begin at i and end at various indices j (where j ≥ i). We do this by iteratively extending the subarray: start with an empty sum (0), then for each new ending index j, add the element at position j to the running sum. This running sum represents the sum of subarray arr[i..j]. After computing each sum, we can compare it with the current maximum. The algorithm takes an array arr and a starting index i as input. We initialize current_sum to 0. Then for j from i to n-1 (where n is array length), we add arr[j] to current_sum. At each step, current_sum holds the sum of arr[i..j]. We can optionally track the maximum of these sums. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in the worst case (when i=0), as we may iterate through the entire array. Space complexity is O(1). This subroutine connects to L0 by being called for each starting index i in the outer loop of the complete solution. In L0, we call this subroutine n times (once for each i), leading to O(n²) total time. Common mistakes include not resetting current_sum between different starting indices or incorrectly handling the loop bounds. The real code pattern involves a simple loop that accumulates sums.

examples.trace:
Array: [1, -2, 3, 4], n=4, i=1
Initialize current_sum = 0, max_from_i = -inf
j=1: current_sum = -2, max_from_i = -2
j=2: current_sum = 1, max_from_i = 1
j=3: current_sum = 5, max_from_i = 5
Maximum sum of subarrays starting at index 1 is 5 (subarray [-2, 3, 4])

Array: [-5, -1, -3], n=3, i=0
Initialize current_sum = 0, max_from_i = -inf
j=0: current_sum = -5, max_from_i = -5
j=1: current_sum = -6, max_from_i = -5
j=2: current_sum = -9, max_from_i = -5
Maximum sum of subarrays starting at index 0 is -5 (subarray [-5])

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Contiguous Subarrays Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays by varying start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is to systematically enumerate all contiguous subarrays of an array. A contiguous subarray is defined by two indices: start (i) and end (j), where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n (n is array length). The intuition is that to find the maximum sum, we must consider every possible such pair (i, j). We use two nested loops: the outer loop iterates over all possible start indices i from 0 to n-1. For each i, the inner loop iterates over all possible end indices j from i to n-1. This ensures we cover all contiguous subarrays exactly once. For example, with array [a, b, c], the subarrays are: [a], [a,b], [a,b,c], [b], [b,c], [c]. The algorithm generates these by: i=0 gives j=0,1,2; i=1 gives j=1,2; i=2 gives j=2. The total number of subarrays is n(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). This logic connects to L1 because for each start index i, we need to handle all end indices j, which is what the L1 subroutine does. Time complexity for this logic alone (without computing sums) is O(n²) due to the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use loop variables. Common mistakes include using incorrect loop bounds (e.g., j starting from 0 instead of i), which would generate non-contiguous or duplicate subarrays, or missing the case when i=j (single-element subarrays). The real code pattern involves simple for-loops with i from 0 to n-1 and j from i to n-1.

examples.trace:
n=3
i=0:
  j=0: subarray [10]
  j=1: subarray [10,20]
  j=2: subarray [10,20,30]
i=1:
  j=1: subarray [20]
  j=2: subarray [20,30]
i=2:
  j=2: subarray [30]
Total 6 subarrays

n=2
i=0:
  j=0: subarray [5]
  j=1: subarray [5,-5]
i=1:
  j=1: subarray [-5]
Total 3 subarrays

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Sum of a Contiguous Subarray Given Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of elements between two indices i and j (inclusive).
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for the brute force approach: given a contiguous subarray defined by start index i and end index j, compute its sum by adding all elements from arr[i] to arr[j]. The intuition is that the sum of a subarray is the total of its elements, which we find by iterating through the indices from i to j and accumulating values. The algorithm takes an array arr, start index i, and end index j as input. We first validate that 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n, where n is array length. Then initialize sum = 0. Loop through index k from i to j (inclusive), and for each k, add arr[k] to sum. Finally, return sum. Time complexity is O(j-i+1), which in the worst case is O(n) when the subarray spans the entire array. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 because in the core logic, we generate all (i,j) pairs, and for each pair, we need to compute the sum using this building block. In the full brute force solution (L0), this operation is performed inside the nested loops. However, in the optimized version (L1), we compute sums incrementally to avoid O(n) work per subarray. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., excluding j), using incorrect loop bounds, or not handling empty subarrays (i>j). The real code pattern is a simple for-loop accumulator.

examples.trace:
Array: [4, 2, 7, 1], i=1, j=3
Initialize sum = 0
k=1: sum = 2
k=2: sum = 9
k=3: sum = 10
Sum of arr[1..3] = 2+7+1 = 10

Array: [-3, 5, -2], i=0, j=2
Initialize sum = 0
k=0: sum = -3
k=1: sum = 2
k=2: sum = 0
Sum of arr[0..2] = -3+5-2 = 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Array Elements with a Loop
ladders.desc: Traverse an array using a loop to access each element sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to array manipulation: using a loop to visit each element of an array in order. The intuition is that arrays are stored contiguously in memory, so we can access elements by their indices. A loop allows us to perform operations on each element systematically. The algorithm involves initializing an index variable (e.g., i) to 0, then repeatedly executing a block of code that uses arr[i], and incrementing i until it reaches the end of the array (length n). In pseudocode: for i from 0 to n-1, do something with arr[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the entire array. Space complexity is O(1) if we only use the loop variable. This connects to L3 because to compute the sum of a subarray, we need to iterate through the elements from i to j using such a loop. In the brute force approach, this operation is used in multiple places: to traverse start indices, end indices, and within subarrays. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= n instead of i < n), modifying the loop variable incorrectly, or not handling empty arrays. The real code pattern is a simple for-loop or while-loop that increments an index.

examples.trace:
Array length n=3
Initialize i=0
i=0: access arr[0]=5
i=1: access arr[1]=3
i=2: access arr[2]=8
i=3: stop (i >= n)

Array length n=2
Initialize i=0
i=0: access arr[0]=10
i=1: access arr[1]=20
i=2: stop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Contiguous Subarray?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a contiguous subarray within an array.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept defines what a contiguous subarray is: a sequence of elements from an array that are consecutive in memory, without any gaps. Formally, given an array arr of length n, a contiguous subarray is defined by two indices i and j (with 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n), and consists of the elements arr[i], arr[i+1], ..., arr[j]. The intuition is that it's a 'slice' of the original array, maintaining the original order and adjacency. For example, in array [a, b, c, d], some contiguous subarrays are [a], [a,b], [b,c,d], but [a,c] is not contiguous because it skips b. Key properties: it must have at least one element (when i=j), it can be the entire array (i=0, j=n-1), and elements are in the same order as in the original array. This concept is crucial for the maximum subarray sum problem because we are only interested in contiguous segments, not arbitrary subsets. Understanding this helps avoid confusion with non-contiguous subsequences. In the brute force approach, we generate all such contiguous subarrays by varying i and j. Common mistakes include confusing subarrays with subsequences (which can skip elements) or with subsets (which can be in any order). The real code pattern involves thinking in terms of start and end indices. This connects to L4 because to work with subarrays, we need to traverse arrays using loops.

examples.trace:
Array has 3 elements.
Start i=0:
  End j=0: [10]
  End j=1: [10,20]
  End j=2: [10,20,30]
Start i=1:
  End j=1: [20]
  End j=2: [20,30]
Start i=2:
  End j=2: [30]
Total 6 contiguous subarrays.

Array has 2 elements.
Start i=0:
  End j=0: [5]
  End j=1: [5,6]
Start i=1:
  End j=1: [6]
Total 3 contiguous subarrays.

testCases.input: What is the contiguous subarray from index 1 to 2 in [7,8,9]?

approaches.name: Divide and Conquer O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Recursively split the array into halves, compute maximum sums in left, right, and crossing subarrays, then combine results. This method leverages recursion and is useful for understanding algorithmic design.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Subarray Sum via Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n log n) divide and conquer solution for maximum subarray sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete divide and conquer solution for finding the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray. The intuition is that the maximum subarray sum must lie entirely in the left half, entirely in the right half, or crossing the midpoint. By recursively solving for left and right halves and computing the crossing sum, we can combine these three possibilities to find the global maximum. The algorithm works by: 1) Base case: if array has one element, return that element. 2) Recursively find maximum sum in left half. 3) Recursively find maximum sum in right half. 4) Find maximum sum crossing the midpoint by extending from midpoint to left and right. 5) Return maximum of these three values. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to the recursion tree with log n levels, each requiring O(n) work for crossing sums. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack. This approach teaches elegant recursive decomposition but is less efficient than Kadane's algorithm. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases, incorrect midpoint calculation, not handling negative numbers properly in crossing sum, and off-by-one errors in array indices. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates all sub-routines into a complete solution.

examples.trace:
Array: [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4]
1. Split at index 4 (midpoint). Left half: [-2, 1, -3, 4], Right half: [-1, 2, 1, -5, 4]
2. Recursively solve left half [-2, 1, -3, 4]:
   - Split at index 2. Left: [-2, 1], Right: [-3, 4]
   - Solve left [-2, 1]: Split at index 1. Left: [-2] returns -2, Right: [1] returns 1, Crossing from index 0-1: max(-2+1, 1) = 1. Max of (-2, 1, 1) = 1
   - Solve right [-3, 4]: Split at index 1. Left: [-3] returns -3, Right: [4] returns 4, Crossing: max(-3+4, 4) = 4. Max of (-3, 4, 4) = 4
   - Crossing for [-2, 1, -3, 4] from index 0-3: Left max from mid: max(-2, -2+1, -2+1-3, -2+1-3+4) = max(-2, -1, -4, 0) = 0. Right max from mid: max(4, 4-1, 4-1+2, 4-1+2+1) = max(4, 3, 5, 6) = 6. Crossing sum = 0 + 6 = 6. Max of (1, 4, 6) = 6
3. Recursively solve right half [-1, 2, 1, -5, 4]:
   - Split at index 2. Left: [-1, 2], Right: [1, -5, 4]
   - Solve left [-1, 2]: Left: [-1] returns -1, Right: [2] returns 2, Crossing: max(-1+2, 2) = 2. Max of (-1, 2, 2) = 2
   - Solve right [1, -5, 4]: Split at index 1. Left: [1], Right: [-5, 4]
     - Solve [-5, 4]: Left: [-5] returns -5, Right: [4] returns 4, Crossing: max(-5+4, 4) = 4. Max of (-5, 4, 4) = 4
     - Crossing for [1, -5, 4]: Left max from mid: max(1, 1-5) = max(1, -4) = 1. Right max from mid: max(4, 4) = 4. Crossing sum = 1 + 4 = 5. Max of (1, 4, 5) = 5
   - Crossing for [-1, 2, 1, -5, 4] from index 0-4: Left max from mid: max(-1, -1+2) = max(-1, 1) = 1. Right max from mid: max(1, 1-5, 1-5+4) = max(1, -4, 0) = 1. Crossing sum = 1 + 1 = 2. Max of (2, 5, 2) = 5
4. Crossing for full array from index 0-8: Left max from mid: max(4, 4-1, 4-1+2, 4-1+2+1) = max(4, 3, 5, 6) = 6. Right max from mid: max(-5, -5+4) = max(-5, -1) = -1. Crossing sum = 6 + (-1) = 5
5. Final max of left max (6), right max (5), crossing (5) = 6

Array: [1, -2, 3, -1, 2]
1. Split at index 2 (midpoint). Left: [1, -2], Right: [3, -1, 2]
2. Solve left [1, -2]: Left: [1] returns 1, Right: [-2] returns -2, Crossing: max(1-2, -2) = max(-1, -2) = -1. Max of (1, -2, -1) = 1
3. Solve right [3, -1, 2]: Split at index 1. Left: [3], Right: [-1, 2]
   - Solve [-1, 2]: Left: [-1] returns -1, Right: [2] returns 2, Crossing: max(-1+2, 2) = max(1, 2) = 2. Max of (-1, 2, 2) = 2
   - Crossing for [3, -1, 2]: Left max from mid: max(3, 3-1) = max(3, 2) = 3. Right max from mid: max(2, 2) = 2. Crossing sum = 3 + 2 = 5. Max of (3, 2, 5) = 5
4. Crossing for full array: Left max from mid: max(-2, -2+3) = max(-2, 1) = 1. Right max from mid: max(-1, -1+2) = max(-1, 1) = 1. Crossing sum = 1 + 1 = 2
5. Final max of left max (1), right max (5), crossing (2) = 5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maximum Crossing Subarray Sum
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum sum of a subarray that must cross a given midpoint.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray that must include elements from both sides of a specified midpoint. The intuition is that by fixing the midpoint, we can extend leftwards and rightwards to find the best possible crossing sum. The algorithm: Given array A, left index low, midpoint mid, right index high: 1) Initialize left_sum to negative infinity and sum to 0. 2) Iterate from mid down to low: add A[i] to sum, update left_sum to max(left_sum, sum). 3) Reset sum to 0, initialize right_sum to negative infinity. 4) Iterate from mid+1 to high: add A[i] to sum, update right_sum to max(right_sum, sum). 5) Return left_sum + right_sum. Time complexity is O(n) where n = high-low+1, as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1). This is the most computationally expensive part of the divide and conquer approach. Common mistakes: using 0 instead of negative infinity for initialization (fails with all negative numbers), incorrect iteration boundaries, not resetting sum between left and right scans. The connection to L0 is that this function is called at each recursive step to compute the crossing case, which is then combined with left and right recursive results.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize left_sum = -inf, sum = 0
2. Left scan from i=mid=1 down to low=0:
   i=1: sum = 0 + (-1) = -1, left_sum = max(-inf, -1) = -1
   i=0: sum = -1 + 4 = 3, left_sum = max(-1, 3) = 3
3. Reset sum = 0, right_sum = -inf
4. Right scan from i=mid+1=2 to high=3:
   i=2: sum = 0 + 2 = 2, right_sum = max(-inf, 2) = 2
   i=3: sum = 2 + 1 = 3, right_sum = max(2, 3) = 3
5. Crossing sum = left_sum + right_sum = 3 + 3 = 6

1. left_sum = -inf, sum = 0
2. Left scan from i=1 to 0:
   i=1: sum = 0 + (-3) = -3, left_sum = max(-inf, -3) = -3
   i=0: sum = -3 + (-2) = -5, left_sum = max(-3, -5) = -3
3. sum = 0, right_sum = -inf
4. Right scan from i=2 to 2:
   i=2: sum = 0 + (-1) = -1, right_sum = max(-inf, -1) = -1
5. Crossing sum = -3 + (-1) = -4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition of Maximum Subarray Problem
ladders.desc: The recursive strategy of splitting problem into left, right, and crossing cases.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive logic without implementation details. The intuition is that any maximum subarray must be entirely in the left half, entirely in the right half, or must cross the midpoint. This three-case decomposition allows us to solve smaller subproblems recursively. The algorithm: 1) If array has one element, return it (base case). 2) Find midpoint. 3) Recursively find maximum sum in left subarray. 4) Recursively find maximum sum in right subarray. 5) Find maximum sum crossing the midpoint (using a helper). 6) Return maximum of these three values. Time complexity analysis: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), which solves to O(n log n) by Master Theorem. Space complexity is O(log n) for recursion stack. The key insight is that the crossing case ensures we don't miss subarrays spanning both halves. Common mistakes: incorrect base case handling, not considering all three cases, misunderstanding recursion termination. The connection to L1 is that this level defines how the crossing sum sub-routine fits into the recursive structure.

examples.trace:
1. Array has 3 elements, not base case
2. Midpoint = index 1
3. Left half: [1, -3]
   - Recursively solve [1, -3]: midpoint = index 0
   - Left of [1, -3]: [1] returns 1 (base case)
   - Right of [1, -3]: [-3] returns -3 (base case)
   - Crossing of [1, -3]: from index 0-1, left max from mid: max(1) = 1, right max from mid: max(-3) = -3, sum = -2
   - Max of (1, -3, -2) = 1
4. Right half: [2] returns 2 (base case)
5. Crossing for full array: from index 0-2, left max from mid: max(-3, -3+1) = max(-3, -2) = -2, right max from mid: max(2) = 2, sum = 0
6. Final max of left max (1), right max (2), crossing (0) = 2

1. Not base case, midpoint = index 1
2. Left half: [-2] returns -2
3. Right half: [-1] returns -1
4. Crossing: from index 0-2, left max from mid: max(5, 5-2) = max(5, 3) = 5, right max from mid: max(-1) = -1, sum = 4
5. Max of (-2, -1, 4) = 4

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum Sum from a Fixed Point
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum sum of a subarray starting or ending at a specific index.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for computing crossing sums. The intuition is that to find the best crossing subarray through a midpoint, we need to find the best possible extension to the left (ending at midpoint) and to the right (starting from midpoint+1). The algorithm for maximum sum ending at index i: initialize max_ending_here = A[0], then for each subsequent element, max_ending_here = max(A[i], max_ending_here + A[i]). For maximum sum starting at index i: similar but iterate backwards. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the array. Space complexity is O(1). This is a simplified version of Kadane's algorithm applied from a fixed point. Common mistakes: incorrect initialization, off-by-one errors in iteration direction, not handling negative numbers correctly. The connection to L2 is that this building block is used twice (left and right scans) within the crossing sum computation.

examples.input: Array: [4, -1, 2, 1], find max sum ending at index 2
examples.trace:
We want maximum sum of subarray ending at index 2 (value 2)
Approach: Consider all subarrays ending at index 2:
- [2] sum = 2
- [-1, 2] sum = 1
- [4, -1, 2] sum = 5
Maximum is 5
Algorithm trace:
Start at index 0: max_ending = 4
Index 1: max_ending = max(-1, 4 + (-1)) = max(-1, 3) = 3
Index 2: max_ending = max(2, 3 + 2) = max(2, 5) = 5

examples.input: Array: [-2, -3, -1], find max sum starting at index 1
examples.trace:
Find maximum sum of subarray starting at index 1 (value -3)
Possible subarrays:
- [-3] sum = -3
- [-3, -1] sum = -4
Maximum is -3
Algorithm trace (iterating forward from index 1):
Start at index 1: max_starting = -3
Index 2: max_starting = max(-1, -3 + (-1)) = max(-1, -4) = -1

testCases.input: Array: [1, 2, 3], find max sum ending at index 2
Array: [-1, -2, -3], find max sum starting at index 0
Array: [5, -2, 3], find max sum ending at index 1
Array: [0, 0, 0], find max sum starting at index 1
Array: [2, -1, 4, -3], find max sum ending at index 3
Array: [10], find max sum ending at index 0
Array: [-5, -1, -3], find max sum starting at index 2
Array: [1, -2, 3], find max sum ending at index 1
Array: [3, 7, -10, 4], find max sum starting at index 2
Array: [4, -1, 7], find max sum ending at index 2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Splitting and Midpoint Calculation
ladders.desc: Divide an array into two halves and compute the midpoint index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial for divide and conquer algorithms. The intuition is that to recursively solve a problem on an array, we need to split it into smaller subarrays. The algorithm: given array A with indices low and high, compute mid = floor((low + high) / 2). The left subarray is A[low..mid] and the right subarray is A[mid+1..high]. Time complexity is O(1) for index calculation. Space complexity is O(1) if we use indices rather than creating new arrays. This operation ensures balanced recursion trees. Common mistakes: incorrect midpoint calculation (off-by-one errors), creating unnecessary array copies (wasting memory), misunderstanding inclusive/exclusive boundaries. The connection to L3 is that this splitting operation enables the recursive decomposition needed for the maximum subarray problem.

examples.trace:
Array has 5 elements (indices 0 through 4)
mid = floor((0 + 4) / 2) = floor(4/2) = floor(2) = 2
Left subarray: indices 0 to 2 inclusive (3 elements)
Right subarray: indices 3 to 4 inclusive (2 elements)

mid = floor((2 + 5) / 2) = floor(7/2) = floor(3.5) = 3
Left: indices 2 to 3 (2 elements)
Right: indices 4 to 5 (2 elements)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subarrays and Contiguous Sequences
ladders.desc: Identify what constitutes a contiguous subarray within an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the entire problem. A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements from the original array. The intuition is that unlike subsets, subarrays must maintain the original order and be consecutive. For example, in array [a, b, c, d], valid subarrays include [a], [b, c], [a, b, c, d], but not [a, c] (not contiguous). The number of subarrays in an array of length n is n(n+1)/2. This concept is essential because the maximum subarray sum problem specifically looks for contiguous sequences, not arbitrary subsets. Common mistakes: confusing subarrays with subsequences (which don't need to be contiguous) or subsets (which don't need to maintain order). Understanding this distinction is critical before attempting any maximum subarray algorithm. The connection to L4 is that once we understand what a subarray is, we can understand why splitting the array at a midpoint affects which subarrays we consider.

examples.trace:
Length 3 array has 3(3+1)/2 = 6 subarrays:
Length 1: [1], [2], [3]
Length 2: [1,2], [2,3]
Length 3: [1,2,3]
Note: [1,3] is NOT a subarray because elements are not contiguous

Length 2 array has 3 subarrays:
[4], [-1], [4,-1]

testCases.input: Number of subarrays in length 4 array

approaches.name: Kadane's Algorithm O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, maintaining a current sum that resets to zero when negative, and track the maximum sum encountered. This is the most efficient and widely used solution for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Subarray Sum with Kadane's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Find the contiguous subarray with the maximum sum in an integer array using Kadane's O(n) algorithm.
ladders.explanation: Kadane's algorithm solves the maximum subarray sum problem optimally in O(n) time and O(1) space. The intuition is that at each position, we decide whether to extend the current subarray or start fresh. We maintain two variables: current_sum (maximum sum ending at current position) and max_sum (overall maximum found). For each element, current_sum = max(element, current_sum + element) — either start a new subarray at this element or extend the previous one. Then update max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum). This works because any maximum subarray must end at some index, and we efficiently compute the best ending at each index. The algorithm handles negative numbers by resetting current_sum when it becomes negative (since adding negative to a subarray reduces its sum). Time is O(n) as we traverse once; space is O(1) using only two variables. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of computing maximum ending at each index. Common mistakes include initializing max_sum to 0 (fails for all-negative arrays — should use -infinity or first element) and misunderstanding the reset condition. Real code pattern: Initialize max_sum = -inf, current_sum = 0. Loop through array: current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num); max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum). Return max_sum.

examples.trace:
Array: [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4]
Initialize: max_sum = -inf, current_sum = 0
Step 1: num = -2, current_sum = max(-2, 0 + -2) = -2, max_sum = max(-inf, -2) = -2
Step 2: num = 1, current_sum = max(1, -2 + 1) = 1, max_sum = max(-2, 1) = 1
Step 3: num = -3, current_sum = max(-3, 1 + -3) = -2, max_sum = max(1, -2) = 1
Step 4: num = 4, current_sum = max(4, -2 + 4) = 4, max_sum = max(1, 4) = 4
Step 5: num = -1, current_sum = max(-1, 4 + -1) = 3, max_sum = max(4, 3) = 4
Step 6: num = 2, current_sum = max(2, 3 + 2) = 5, max_sum = max(4, 5) = 5
Step 7: num = 1, current_sum = max(1, 5 + 1) = 6, max_sum = max(5, 6) = 6
Step 8: num = -5, current_sum = max(-5, 6 + -5) = 1, max_sum = max(6, 1) = 6
Step 9: num = 4, current_sum = max(4, 1 + 4) = 5, max_sum = max(6, 5) = 6
Result: 6 (subarray [4, -1, 2, 1])

Array: [-1, -2, -3, -4]
Initialize: max_sum = -inf, current_sum = 0
Step 1: num = -1, current_sum = max(-1, 0 + -1) = -1, max_sum = max(-inf, -1) = -1
Step 2: num = -2, current_sum = max(-2, -1 + -2) = -2, max_sum = max(-1, -2) = -1
Step 3: num = -3, current_sum = max(-3, -2 + -3) = -3, max_sum = max(-1, -3) = -1
Step 4: num = -4, current_sum = max(-4, -3 + -4) = -4, max_sum = max(-1, -4) = -1
Result: -1 (subarray [-1])

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maximum Sum Ending at Each Index
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum sum of a subarray that must end at each position in the array.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of Kadane's algorithm. For each index i, we find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray that ends exactly at i. The intuition: to maximize the sum ending at i, we either take only the element at i (starting a new subarray) or add it to the best sum ending at i-1 (extending the previous subarray). Formally, let dp[i] = maximum sum ending at i. Then dp[i] = max(arr[i], dp[i-1] + arr[i]). This works because any subarray ending at i either consists solely of arr[i] or extends a subarray ending at i-1; we choose the better option. Time is O(n) as we compute for each index; space is O(n) if storing all dp values, but can be O(1) by keeping only the previous value. Connection to L0: This subroutine directly gives current_sum in Kadane's algorithm, and tracking the maximum over all dp[i] yields the global maximum. Common mistakes include not handling negative dp[i-1] correctly (the formula automatically resets when dp[i-1] is negative, as arr[i] > dp[i-1] + arr[i]). Real code pattern: Initialize prev = 0. For each num in array: current = max(num, prev + num); update prev = current; optionally track max. Example: For [1, -2, 3], dp[0]=1, dp[1]=max(-2, 1-2)=-1, dp[2]=max(3, -1+3)=3.

examples.trace:
Array: [1, -2, 3, -1, 2]
Initialize: prev = 0 (or use dp array)
Index 0: num = 1, current = max(1, 0 + 1) = 1, dp[0] = 1
Index 1: num = -2, current = max(-2, 1 + -2) = -1, dp[1] = -1
Index 2: num = 3, current = max(3, -1 + 3) = 3, dp[2] = 3
Index 3: num = -1, current = max(-1, 3 + -1) = 2, dp[3] = 2
Index 4: num = 2, current = max(2, 2 + 2) = 4, dp[4] = 4
Result: [1, -1, 3, 2, 4]

Array: [-5, -1, -3]
Initialize: prev = 0
Index 0: num = -5, current = max(-5, 0 + -5) = -5, dp[0] = -5
Index 1: num = -1, current = max(-1, -5 + -1) = -1, dp[1] = -1
Index 2: num = -3, current = max(-3, -1 + -3) = -3, dp[2] = -3
Result: [-5, -1, -3]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Local vs Global Maximum Decision
ladders.desc: At each step, decide whether to extend the current subarray or start a new one based on which gives higher sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of Kadane's algorithm: the decision between extending a subarray or starting fresh. For each element, we compare two options: (1) Start a new subarray at this element (sum = element), or (2) Extend the previous subarray by adding this element to the current running sum. We choose the maximum of these two. Intuitively, if the current running sum is negative, adding the element to it will only reduce the element's value, so it's better to discard the past and start anew. This local decision ensures we always have the best possible sum ending at the current position. The logic is encapsulated in current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num). Time is O(1) per element; overall O(n). Space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L1: This decision computes each dp[i] in the maximum-ending-at-index subroutine. Common mistakes include resetting current_sum to 0 when negative (should reset to num, not 0) or not understanding why we compare with num alone. Real code pattern: For each num, if current_sum < 0: current_sum = num else: current_sum += num (this is equivalent to max). Example: With current_sum = -2 and num = 3, max(3, -2+3)=3, so start new.

examples.trace:
Given: current_sum = -2, num = 5
Option 1: Start new subarray with num = 5
Option 2: Extend previous: current_sum + num = -2 + 5 = 3
Compare: max(5, 3) = 5
Decision: Start new, so current_sum = 5

Given: current_sum = 4, num = -1
Option 1: Start new subarray with num = -1
Option 2: Extend previous: current_sum + num = 4 + -1 = 3
Compare: max(-1, 3) = 3
Decision: Extend, so current_sum = 3

ladders.title: Building Block — Running Sum with Reset Condition
ladders.desc: Maintain a running sum of elements, but reset it to zero or a new value when it becomes disadvantageous.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves keeping a cumulative sum as we iterate through a sequence, with a reset mechanism. The running sum adds each new element to the total so far. However, if the running sum becomes negative, it's detrimental to future additions because adding a negative to any number reduces it. Thus, we reset the running sum—either to 0 or to the current element—to avoid carrying negative baggage. In Kadane's, we reset to the current element (since starting a new subarray there). Intuition: A negative running sum indicates that the previous segment is harmful, so we discard it. Time is O(n) for traversal; space is O(1). Connection to L2: This reset condition implements the 'start new' decision when current_sum is negative. Common mistakes include resetting to 0 instead of the element (which works only if we then add the element, but direct reset to element is cleaner) or resetting too early. Real code pattern: running_sum = 0; for num in array: running_sum += num; if running_sum < 0: running_sum = 0 (or num for Kadane's). Example: Array [2, -3, 4]: after 2, sum=2; after -3, sum=-1 (reset to 0); after 4, sum=4.

examples.trace:
Array: [1, -2, 3, -4, 5]
Initialize running_sum = 0
Step 1: num = 1, running_sum = 0 + 1 = 1
Step 2: num = -2, running_sum = 1 + -2 = -1, since -1 < 0, reset to 0
Step 3: num = 3, running_sum = 0 + 3 = 3
Step 4: num = -4, running_sum = 3 + -4 = -1, reset to 0
Step 5: num = 5, running_sum = 0 + 5 = 5
Result: running sums after each step: 1, 0, 3, 0, 5

Array: [-1, 2, -3]
Initialize running_sum = 0
Step 1: num = -1, running_sum = 0 + -1 = -1, reset to 0
Step 2: num = 2, running_sum = 0 + 2 = 2
Step 3: num = -3, running_sum = 2 + -3 = -1, reset to 0
Result: running sums: 0, 2, 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Array Traversal with Sum Update
ladders.desc: Loop through an array, accumulating a sum by adding each element to a running total.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves sequentially processing each element in an array and maintaining a cumulative sum. We start with an initial sum (often 0), then for each element, we add its value to the sum. This is basic iteration and accumulation, foundational for many algorithms. Intuition: By traversing left to right, we build up information incrementally. Time is O(n) for n elements; space is O(1) for the sum variable. Connection to L3: This is the core of maintaining a running sum before adding reset logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or not initializing the sum properly. Real code pattern: sum = 0; for num in array: sum += num; return sum. Example: For [1,2,3], sum starts 0, after 1:1, after 2:3, after 3:6.

examples.trace:
Array: [4, -2, 3, 1]
Initialize sum = 0
Step 1: num = 4, sum = 0 + 4 = 4
Step 2: num = -2, sum = 4 + (-2) = 2
Step 3: num = 3, sum = 2 + 3 = 5
Step 4: num = 1, sum = 5 + 1 = 6
Result: 6

Array: [0, 5, -3, 2]
Initialize sum = 0
Step 1: num = 0, sum = 0 + 0 = 0
Step 2: num = 5, sum = 0 + 5 = 5
Step 3: num = -3, sum = 5 + (-3) = 2
Step 4: num = 2, sum = 2 + 2 = 4
Result: 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Simple Loops
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array sequentially using a loop and perform basic arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: working with arrays and loops. An array is a collection of elements accessible by index. A loop (e.g., for or while) allows us to visit each element one by one. We combine this with arithmetic operations like addition to accumulate values. Intuition: By iterating, we can process data step-by-step. For beginners, this involves understanding zero-based indexing, loop initialization, condition, and update. Time depends on number of elements; space is minimal. Connection to L4: This foundational skill enables iterative traversal for sum accumulation. Common mistakes include infinite loops, index out-of-bounds, or misunderstanding loop scope. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; total = 0; for i in range(len(arr)): total = total + arr[i]; print(total). Example: For arr [2,3], loop i=0: total=0+2=2; i=1: total=2+3=5.

examples.trace:
Array: [2, 4, 6]
Initialize total = 0
Loop index 0: element = 2, total = 0 + 2 = 2
Loop index 1: element = 4, total = 2 + 4 = 6
Loop index 2: element = 6, total = 6 + 6 = 12
Result: 12

Array: [1, -1, 2]
Initialize total = 0
Loop index 0: element = 1, total = 0 + 1 = 1
Loop index 1: element = -1, total = 1 + (-1) = 0
Loop index 2: element = 2, total = 0 + 2 = 2
Result: 2
